interactions.summary: Jiang frames the second game-theory class as a training exercise in seeing everyday life as a game. School officially exists to create functional, creative people through literacy, core competencies, and lifelong learning, but most schools fail so badly that they make students hate reading, collaboration, thinking, and learning.

He turns to his own 2008 school-reform story: after studying at Yale he was hired to build an international program in South China, found a test-and-memorization culture, and introduced seminars, books, a large English library, and practical student-run activities.

claims.claim: Jiang defines the game-theory course goal as learning to analyze why events happen and predict how they develop by seeing the motivations behind behavior.
He says school is supposed to train students for social function and creativity through literacy, core competencies, and lifelong learning.

He argues that most schools do not merely fail to teach these purposes; they produce the opposite effect by weakening reading, turning collaboration into zero-sum competition, and making students hate learning.

In Jiang's account, the 2008 South China program began from replacing memorization and performative activities with seminar discussion, real reading, student-run business practice, and doing rather than merely talking.

signature_moments.moment: School is supposed to teach lifelong learning, but the ritual ending is students burning books as liberation.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It condenses Jiang's indictment: the institution meant to produce learning becomes a machine for hating learning.

signature_moments.moment: His reform principle is blunt: stop having students sit around talking and make them do things.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I wanted to let them do things
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This becomes the practical contrast between institutional signaling and real competence.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang uses the term for skills needed in life, especially critical thinking, cooperation, collaboration, and communication.
A constant process of learning required because AI, globalization, and social change keep remaking the world.
Jiang's description of school competition where getting ahead means beating or 'schooling' classmates rather than collaborating with them.

chronology_notes.note: The lecture anchors the reform story in 2008, after Jiang's Yale College period and before the international-school market had many competitors.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR appears to render the school name and location imperfectly; the broader 2008 South China international-program setup is still clear.
